nativeRange.habitat: Open sand and rubble zones adjacent to reefs, typically at depths of 15–37 m. Found hovering near shared pistol-shrimp burrow entrances.

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate at least 5 cm deep for burrow construction
loose coral rubble or flat rock pieces near the sand for burrow anchoring
open sandy footprint near the front glass for visibility
low-flow zone around the burrow entrance

diet.notes: The Dracula Goby is a carnivore that generally accepts frozen mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped shrimp or fish flesh, krill, and small carnivore pellets or flakes. Feeding twice daily in small portions is commonly advised. Newly imported specimens have been reported to take time to accept prepared foods; offering live or frozen items first tends to trigger the feeding response.

goodWith: pistol shrimp (Alpheus randalli — documented partner species)
small peaceful reef fish (firefish, cardinalfish, royal grammas)
avoidWith: large aggressive predators (groupers, lionfish, large angelfish)
boisterous bottom-dwellers that disturb the burrow
other Stonogobiops or shrimp gobies competing for the same sandy territory
triggerfish and other crustacean-eating species (will prey on the partner shrimp)

imageCandidates.notes: Sharp, well-lit full-body live specimen on coral sand; clearly shows pale white body, dark-red/maroon vertical bands, yellow head and oversized eye. 3792x2324.

summary: A rare and striking shrimp goby with a pale body, dark-red banding, and oversized eyes. Forms a symbiotic burrow partnership with Randall's pistol shrimp on sand and rubble near reefs.
careNotes:
The Dracula Goby is among the rarer Stonogobiops shrimp gobies in the hobby, prized for its dramatic red-banded coloration and large, expressive eyes. Like its congeners, it naturally pairs with a pistol shrimp — documented with Alpheus randalli in the wild — which excavates and maintains a shared burrow while the goby stands sentry, watching for predators and food.

A sand bed of at least 5 cm depth and a few flat rubble pieces for the shrimp to build under are generally advised. An open sandy footprint near the front of the tank lets the pair be observed easily.

A tightly-fitting lid is strongly advised — like most gobies and dartfish, this species has been known to jump when startled or during acclimation. The Dracula Goby is generally considered fully reef-safe and has not been reported to nip corals or target ornamental invertebrates, though sand-sifting activity can occasionally bury low-placed frags.

This species is noted as rare and expensive in the trade, and captive-bred specimens are not currently available. Introducing the goby and its pistol shrimp partner simultaneously into the aquarium is commonly recommended to encourage pairing.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page navigated from the gobies collection root. Primary hobby source for water parameters, tank size minimum, temperament, diet, reef safety, and care level.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Gobiidae, order Gobiiformes), maximum adult size (7.0 cm TL), wild depth range (15–37 m), native range (Seychelles, Maldives, Western Indian Ocean), wild temperature range (20–28.2°C), and symbiotic association with Alpheus randalli. FishBase temperature ceiling is higher than the LiveAquaria hobby range; averaged to 27°C max.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Third cross-verification source. Confirmed size range (6–7 cm), temperature (22–28°C), minimum tank of ~100 L (22 gal), and carnivore diet (mysis, brine shrimp, krill, frozen). Rated care level 'advanced only', higher than LiveAquaria's 'moderate'; averaged to 'intermediate' in the final entry. Confirms species is rare and not captive-bred.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy confirmation (Gobiidae / Gobiiformes) and native range corroboration (Seychelles, Maldives). Described 1977 by Polunin and Lubbock.
